title: It Takes Two to Borrow: The Effects of the Equal Credit Opportunity Act on Housing, Credit, and Labor Market Decisions of Married Couples
abstract: Until the 1970s, U.S. mortgage lenders commonly discounted half of the wife’s income in couples’ joint mortgage applications. This changed with the introduction of antidiscrimination legislation in the 1970s, providing a natural experiment to study the relaxation of income-related borrowing constraints. I study the effects of the reform by estimating difference-in-differences regressions and solving a simple calibrated life cycle model. I find substantial positive effects of the reform on mortgage borrowing and homeownership rates of married couples with working wives. Moreover, I find a positive effect on married women’s labor force participation, which strongly amplifies the homeownership and borrowing effects.

article.1. Historical Context:
JEL E21 - Consumption; Saving; Wealth J16 - Economics of Gender; Non-labor Discrimination J18 - Public Policy N32 - U.S.; Canada: 1913- R21 - Housing Demand R38 - Government Policy Issue Section: Articles Associate Editor: Tarun Ramadorai Tarun Ramadorai Associate Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Access to credit is essential for economic participation and consumption smoothing. Yet only half a century ago, credit access was substantially restricted for a large share of the United States’ citizens. Until the 1970s, discrimination in credit transactions based on sex and marital status was widespread and institutionalized. Married women faced particularly severe restrictions. Most importantly, common practice was to discount the wife’s income by 50%when a couple jointly applied for a mortgage or even completely disregard it (Kendig 1973). This was crucial because mortgage debt was and is the dominant form of household debt in the United States, accounting for about 80%of household borrowing (Bartscher et al. 2021). Female labor force participation had increased substantially by the 1970s and kept rising, particularly among married women (Juhn and Potter 2006). With stable female employment becoming ever more common, the practice of income discounting increasingly failed to live up to married couples’ economic needs and capacities.

In the early 1970s, not a single country in the world had a law prohibiting gender-based discrimination in access to credit (seeFigure 1). This changed when the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA) was passed in the United States in 1974, becoming effective in 1975 (Smith 1977). The new law precluded discrimination in lending based on sex and marital status, including income discounting. It thus constitutes a natural experiment to study to what extent the relaxation of debt-to-income (DTI) constraints affects households’ access to mortgage credit and homeownership. While the macro-financial literature has mainly focused on loan-to-value (LTV) constraints to model frictional financial markets, DTI constraints have only recently gained more attention and deserve further study (Greenwald 2018). Moreover, given that the relaxation of the constraint worked directly via the creditable share of the wife’s income, the reform also allows to investigate interactions between mortgage markets and female labor supply.

I apply difference-in-differences and event study techniques to show that the ECOA had sizable and significant positive effects on the homeownership and mortgage borrowing rates of married couples with working wives. These results provide clear evidence in support of the importance of DTI constraints. Moreover, I find evidence of positive labor supply responses among young married women. To strengthen the evidence, I verify that the mechanism suspected to drive the empirical results—a relaxation of the DTI constraint operating on the wife’s income—can generate similar effects in a simple quantitative life cycle model. The model speaks in favor of an important substitution effect: by increasing the creditable share of married women’s incomes, the ECOA raised their return to labor supply, thus motivating them to work. The increased labor supply in turn amplifies the positive effects on homeownership and borrowing.

In the empirical part of the paper, I use data from the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) to estimate difference-in-differences regressions at the federal level and event studies at the state level. I focus on married couples with working wives, who could immediately benefit from the reform upon its implementation. As the ECOA prohibited income discounting, I hypothesize that households with a higher income contribution of the wife could benefit more. I thus start by comparing households with a higher to those with a lower female income contribution before and after the reform in difference-in-differences regressions at the federal level. My results show that after the ECOA, the increase in mortgage DTI ratios was higher the more the wife’s earnings had contributed to her household’s total income in the pre-reform years. Before the reform, there were no differential trends in DTI ratios depending on the wife’s income share. The effect on mortgage debt is mainly driven by the extensive margin, that is, a higher relative share of households holding a mortgage. Additionally, I find a relative increase in homeownership, housing-to-income (HTI) ratios, and home size. My estimates imply that the introduction of legislation against gender-based credit discrimination allowed 1.4 million married households to move to an own home.

The first congressional hearings on equal credit opportunity for women, which finally led to the passage of the ECOA, took place in 1972.Figure A.3in theInternet Appendixillustrates how attention for the topic “women and credit” surged from 1972 on. Against this background, I use 1971 as the last pre-reform year in the federal-level difference-in-differences regressions. Another important aspect is that several states already introduced equal credit opportunity laws on their own initiative in the period between the congressional hearings of 1972 and the ECOA’s effective date in 1975 (U.S. Department of Labor. 1975). This allows me to exploit state-level variation in an event study design. For this purpose, I collected information on the respective state laws.

While the national-level regressions rely on a comparison of households with higher versus lower female income contributions within a state, the event study design compares households with a working wife in states which have already implemented an anti-lending-discrimination law in a given year to similar households in states that have not done so yet. I find positive and significant effects on homeownership, house size and HTI ratios of married couples in treated relative to untreated states after a reform.1There were no differential trends across states during the pre-reform years. Hence, the results corroborate the national-level evidence based on state-level variation. An important strength of the event studies is that they are unlikely to pick up other contemporaneous events, given that these would have to coincide geographically and temporally with the introduction of the state-level laws. I further conduct a battery of robustness checks for the national-level results to minimize the risk that they are driven by confounding events.

On impact, the new legislation was most beneficial for households in which the wife had already been working, as lenders were still allowed to take employment continuity into account when determining mortgage eligibility (Geary 1976). However, the ECOA may have changed the labor supply incentives for women in subsequent years. There can be opposing income and substitution effects on female labor supply. On the one hand, a wife ceteris paribus had to work less after the reform to afford a given mortgage. On the other hand, her return to labor supply in terms of borrowing capacity increased, providing positive work incentives. Based on event studies, I find evidence that young married women increased their workforce participation in response to the introduction of anti-lending-discrimination laws. The effects are however estimated with some uncertainty, as they rely on a relatively small number of households.

Changes in labor supply incentives affect not only existing households but also couples who only form a household and make their labor market and housing decisions after the reform. These households are not captured by comparisons between pre- and post-reform periods in the data. Ideally, we would like to know how they would have reacted in an alternative world in which the reform was never implemented, but this counterfactual remains unobserved. I thus build a life cycle model of married households’ homeownership and mortgage choices, extending previous work by researchers likePizzinelli (2018)andAttanasio et al. (2012). The model allows me to run counterfactual simulations and assess whether the empirical estimates are plausible from a theoretical perspective.

Couples in the model face idiosyncratic income risk and can choose whether to rent or own a house. Owners can borrow against their house subject to an LTV and a DTI constraint. Mortgages are modeled as long-term debt. I calibrate the model to the early 1970s, and simulate it under the assumption that either 50%or 100%of the wife’s income can be counted toward a mortgage, which reflects the change in lending practices induced by the ECOA. Since the model allows to run a clean controlled experiment, the comparison of these two alternative scenarios allows to draw causal conclusions on the effects of abolishing income discounting.

I first look at homeownership and borrowing responses of households whose wife made her labor supply decisions in a world with income discounting, and show that the model can endogenously generate effects that are close to those from the natural experiment in the data. Moreover, the model results support the idea that the ECOA encouraged married women to work. In line with the empirical evidence, the abolition of income discounting entails an increase in married women’s workforce participation of around 2 percentage points. The additional female labor supply approximately doubles the effects on homeownership and borrowing. While the new equal credit opportunity legislation already had sizable positive effects on married couple’s homeownership and borrowing upon its introduction, the model suggests even larger medium- to long-run effects due to the amplification via higher female labor force participation, as more households can benefit from the possibility to count the wife’s full income toward a mortgage.

Although the ECOA required creditors to profoundly change their lending practices (Smith 1977), its effect on women’s access to credit have remained unclear.Elliehausen and Durkin (1989)argue theoretically why they think the ECOA did not increase credit availability “to anyone.”Ladd (1982)andHaurin and Kamara (1992)provide suggestive correlational evidence pointing to the contrary. Yet none of these studies conduct a comprehensive econometric analysis. My paper fills this gap, finding that the ECOA indeed improved credit access for married women and their households.

Looking beyond the specific reform under study, my paper contributes to the literature on gender and household finance. For instance,Hazan, Weiss, and Zoabi (2019)show that the extension of married women’s property rights to movable property in the United States since the 1850s induced significant shifts in household portfolios.Goldsmith-Pinkham and Shue (forthcoming)find that women earn smaller returns on housing than men, mainly because of differences in market timing but potentially also because of discrimination in negotiations.2Bucher-Koenen et al. (2021)demonstrate that women are more likely to get worse financial advice, resulting in higher investment costs.

Several studies have shown that even in modern times, women encounter more difficulties in obtaining business and consumer loans than comparable men in emerging and advanced countries (Morazzoni and Sy 2022;Brock and De Haas forthcoming;Montoya et al. 2021).Giacoletti, Heimer, and Yu (2022)find a significant gap between the approval rates of black versus white mortgage applicants in the United States. This gap is substantially reduced at the end of a month, when loan officers have less incentives for a disparate treatment of applicants as they have to fulfill volume quotas. The authors find similar patterns for female versus male applicants. A common ground of these studies is that loan officers’ discretion still plays an important role today and can lead to unequal treatment of applicants. Recent work further shows that the increased proliferation of algorithmic decision-making in lending does not necessarily eliminate such inequalities, for instance if algorithms allow to proxy for membership in a protected class more effectively (Morse and Pence 2021;Fuster et al. 2022). My research adds to this literature by showing that improving women’s access to mortgage credit has important effects both on household balance sheets and married women’s labor supply decisions.

The paper also adds to the literature on borrowing constraints and their interaction with female labor supply.Greenwald (2018)points out that the importance of DTI limits has remained understudied in the macro-finance literature.Foote, Loewenstein, and Willen (2018)argue that a relaxation of DTI constraints before the Financial Crisis helped to raise homeownership rates, but do not study labor supply. Relaxations of DTI and LTV constraints have different effects on female labor supply incentives. Relaxing LTV constraints allows households to borrow more against their house, which can be used to cushion idiosyncratic income shocks (Herkenhoff, Phillips, and Cohen-Cole 2020). This reduces wives’ labor supply incentives, as there is less need to insure against husbands’ negative income shocks (cf.Attanasio, Low, and Sánchez-Marcos 2005;Pruitt and Turner 2020). By contrast, a loosening of DTI constraints has opposing income and substitution effects on wives’ labor supply, as shown in this paper.

Empirical papers have found negative effects of relaxed LTV constraints and increased credit supply on female labor supply (Kumar and Liang 2019;Del Boca and Lusardi 2003;Dao Bui and Ume 2020). By contrast, women in households with larger mortgages or closer to the DTI constraint are more likely to work, and work more (see, for example,Fortin 1995). My results show that a relaxation of income-related borrowing constraints via the creditable share of the wife’s income can incentivize women to work, and that the increased labor force participation strongly amplifies the positive direct effects of relaxing the constraint on homeownership and borrowing.
Until the 1970s, American women faced various difficulties if they wanted to borrow money (National Commission on Consumer Finance 1972). Single women were often unable to obtain credit, especially mortgages. However, married women had “greater difficulty in obtaining credit than [...] any other women” (Cairns 1976, p. 967). The most common restriction was the practice of discounting the wife’s income by 50%when a couple jointly applied for a mortgage. In certain cases, her income was not counted at all, for example, if the marriage was recent or the couple was young (Kendig 1973). Many creditors applied stricter standards for applications if the wife was the main wage earner, and some required a proof of birth control (Cairns 1976).

These practices would not only often violate women’s privacy but also entail economic disadvantages, since mortgage credit can provide access to better jobs, education, and healthcare through providing the opportunity to move. By the 1970s, credit had become a “necessity for all” in the United States (Cuomo 1981, p. 126). While the economic disadvantages for women and their families were evident, there was little evidence of economic justifications.Internet Appendix A.1provides a detailed discussion. Importantly, female labor force participation had increased considerably by the 1970s (seeFigure A.1ain theInternet Appendix), especially among married women (Juhn and Potter 2006). Of course, women still left the workforce because of pregnancy, but they also returned in increasing numbers (Lally 1974). From a theoretical perspective, it would be difficult to rationalize a woman leaving the labor force for childcare if doing so led to foreclosure. InInternet Appendix A.1, I show that women are more likely to work if their household would approach the debt-service constraint without their income (cf.Fortin 1995). I further show that husbands’ and wives’ labor incomes evolve very similarly in the years after a home purchase, which lenders consider as the riskiest period (Thurston 2018;Foote, Loewenstein, and Willen 2018). Economic studies already suggested that women are no worse or even better credit risks than men in the 1970s (Lally 1974;Cairns 1976), and a group of 180 economists explicitly criticized the prevailing practices as arbitrary in 1972 (Statement of Economists 1972). Nevertheless, lenders made hardly any voluntary attempts to count larger shares of working wives’ incomes prior to the early 1970s.3Major market participants, from the Veterans Administration (VA) and Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs) to commercial mortgage lenders, continued to discount wives’ incomes until the 1970s (Kendig 1973).

In May 1972, the National Commission on Consumer Finance held the first congressional hearings on the availability of credit to women. In response to the hearings and the commission’s recommendations, several states already enacted laws banning discrimination on the basis of sex or marital status in the subsequent years (seeTable B.2in theInternet Appendix). At the federal level, the ECOA was signed into law in October 1974, and became effective in October 1975 (Smith 1977). It required “that financial institutions and other firms engaged in the extension of credit make that credit equally available to all creditworthy customers without regard to sex or marital status” (Public Law 93-495, Title V, §502).4Importantly, the ECOA prescribed substantial penalties for violations,5which were perceived as “adequate to sufficiently deter creditors from willful violations” (Smith 1977, p. 610). It is also important to note that the ECOA only prohibited lenders from discounting the wife’s incomemerelybecause she was a woman. It was still allowed to consider aspects like employment continuity and income stability (Geary 1976;Cairns 1976).

Surprisingly, only a few economists have examined the effects of the ECOA. Some suggestive evidence indicates that the ECOA might have had important positive effects on married women’s access to credit (for a summary, seeInternet Appendix E), but formal econometric evidence was previously missing. In the following, I will explore the effects of the ECOA in a difference-in-differences design. I will use data from the PSID, which allows me to track the same households over time and include pre-reform characteristics in the estimation.
article.2. Data:
The PSID is a widely used representative survey of U.S. families. The survey was conducted annually between 1968 and 1997, and has been continued at a biennial frequency since then. To obtain household-level data, I aggregate PSID families who are living together into one household (cf.Pfeffer et al. 2016). I use data from the PSID’s “Survey Research Center” (SRC) sample, which is nationally representative and tracks households from the first PSID wave over time, as well as the new households formed by former members, for example, adult children moving out.6Survey weights are used for the calculation of descriptive statistics (Hill 1991).7I use the longitudinal PSID family weights and post-stratify them to match age, race, and homeownership from the Current Population Survey (CPS), following the procedure ofKuhn, Schularick, and Steins (2020). All nominal variables have been deflated with the CPI, obtained from theMacrohistory Database(Jordà, Schularick, and Taylor 2017), such that the reported results are in $US(2016).

Before the ECOA, women’s access to credit was restricted both for secured and unsecured debt. Comprehensive information on wealth is available in the PSID only for the years since 1984 and was initially only queried every 5 years. Questions on components of personal debt like student and credit card debt are only available since 2011. However, the majority of household debt in the United States consists of housing debt (Bartscher et al. 2021). Information on the outstanding mortgage balance is available since 1969, as well as information on monthly mortgage payments. Unfortunately, both of these variables are missing for 1973–1975 and 1982. Information on the asset value of houses and their size is available without interruptions since 1968. Moreover, the survey provides information on the labor income of household heads and spouses, as well as total household income. Labor income includes wages and salaries, as well as the labor portion of other income, for example, from businesses. Additionally, the PSID contains a wide range of demographic variables, such as age, number of children, and state of residence.8

My analysis sample starts in 1969, when mortgage data become available. In 1986, the Reagan Tax Reform Act introduced major changes in mortgage markets (Bartscher et al. 2021), and potentially also labor markets (Juhn and Potter 2006;Bastian 2020). Therefore, I will focus on the period until 1985, which corresponds to a period of 10 years after the ECOA’s effective date in 1975. As discussed in Section1, married women arguably had the greatest difficulties of all women to obtain access to credit prior to the ECOA, in particular regarding mortgages. Moreover, even if the share of single households living in an owned and mortgaged property increased after the mid-1970s, it was still comparatively small (seeFigure C.2in theInternet Appendix). Therefore, I will focus my analysis on married households. Accordingly, single households are excluded from the sample. The head is defined as the male partner in all cases. I will refer to this as the baseline sample.

For some complementary results, I also use data from the “SCF+.” This data set combines the modern Survey on Consumer Finances (SCF) with its historical predecessors.Kuhn, Schularick, and Steins (2020)describe this data source in detail.
article.3. Effects of the ECOA:
In the following, I will motivate my hypotheses regarding the effects of the ECOA and provide descriptive evidence. Thereafter, I will discuss how to identify effects of the ECOA in a difference-in-differences setup, and present my estimation results at the federal level. Finally, I will provide evidence for the robustness of the results on homeownership in an event study setup, and examine effects on female labor supply.

3.1 Motivation of hypotheses and descriptive evidence

When the reform was introduced, it could immediately benefit households in which the wife had already been working. Consider a household consisting of a male spouse m and a female spouse f ⁠ , who earn labor incomes y m and y f ⁠ . Let s f denote the share of the wife’s labor income in total household income y ⁠ , such that y f = s f y ⁠ . Before the reform, the mortgage DTI constraint ϕ of this household had the following form: ϕ ( y m , y f ) = λ y ( y m + λ d s f y ) .

λyis the DTI limit, andλddetermines by how much the wife’s income is discounted; that is, it was typically 0.5 before the reform. We can easily see that an increase inλdrelaxes the constraint, and the relaxation will be larger if the wife is contributing a larger sharesfto her household’s total income. Based on these considerations, my first hypothesis is that married households in which the wife contributed a larger share to household income could increase their mortgage borrowing by more in response to the reform, thereby gaining more access to homeownership.

Figure 2provides descriptive evidence in line with this conjecture. It shows the evolution of DTI and HTI ratios for households with different average female income shares in the pre-reform period, that is, before the congressional hearings of 1972. The trends in both DTI and HTI ratios were very similar for all groups of households prior to 1972. However, we see a clear divergence thereafter, with DTI and HTI ratios increasing for households with, relative to those without, a working wife. This relative increase is stronger the more the wife was contributing to her household’s total income before the reform. The faster debt increase of married households with a working wife subsequent to the ECOA suggests that the possibility to fully count the wife’s income toward a mortgage enabled married couples who had previously been restricted by debt-service constraints to borrow and invest in housing. In Section3.2, I will buttress these descriptive results with formally identified difference-in-differences estimates.

Given that the relaxation of the DTI constraint worked directly via the creditable share of the wife’s income, my second hypothesis is that the reform also affected married women’s labor supply.Figure 3shows that the share of married women working was quite stable in the late 1960s and early 1970s, but increased pronouncedly thereafter.9While different factors have contributed to this trend (seeInternet Appendix A.1), it is plausible that the ECOA was among the drivers. From a theoretical perspective, two effects are conceivable, which work in opposite directions. One the one hand, there might be an income effect: to afford a mortgage of a given size, a married woman needed to work less after the reform, all else being equal. On the other hand, there might be a substitution effect: given that the return to each dollar earned by the wife in terms of borrowing capacity increased with the reform, it became more attractive for married women to work.

Figure 3 Share of married households with a working wife Open in new tab Download slide The graph shows the share of married households in which the wife reports positive labor income over time. The data were smoothed by taking a 3-year moving average.

In Section3.3, I will use event study regressions to provide evidence that married women’s workforce participation indeed increased in response to the prohibition of income discounting. When looking at all married households with women of working age, the results are estimated with lower precision. Yet when restricting the sample to households for whom economic theory would suggest particularly strong effects, namely, young households who still have to make their female labor supply and homeownership decisions, the point estimates indeed increase in size and become significant. Given the small sample size, this evidence should however be interpreted with some caution.

The empirical strategy relies on comparisons of existing households between the pre- and post-reform period. However, it remains silent on the question which labor supply and housing decisions those households who were only formed in the post-reform period would have made if there had never been a reform. While I only observe the realized path of history in the data, a theoretical model allows me to simulate an alternative path of history in which there is no reform. In Section4, I therefore build a simple quantitative life cycle model in order to strengthen the empirical evidence. This allows me to compare the life cycle behavior of households living in the model equivalent of the actual world, in which the reform was implemented, to a counterfactual world without reform.

The model corroborates that more married women work in the world with the reform than in the counterfactual world without it, with the effect being of a similar size as suggested by the data. The increase in workforce participation has an amplifying effect on overall homeownership and borrowing, because more households can benefit from the possibility to count two full incomes as collateral toward a mortgage. In line with this reasoning, I find that the extent to which married households relied on the wife’s income for mortgage borrowing increased substantially after the reform (seeInternet Appendix A.3).

3.2 Effects on housing and mortgage borrowing

My first aim is to identify the effects of the ECOA on the mortgage borrowing and homeownership decisions of married households with a working wife. To this end, I estimate simple difference-in-differences regressions. As discussed above, I will treat 1971 as the last pre-reform year, because the first actions toward equal access to credit for women were taken after this year. The empirical specification is as follows: Y i s t = ∑ t = 1969 , t ≠ 1971 1985 β t ⋅ δ t ⋅ s h a r e i p r e + α s t + γ i + Γ ′ X i s t + ϵ i s t . (1)

Yistis the outcome variable, for instance the housing DTI ratio of householdiliving in statesin yeart⁠.δtare year dummies andshareipreis the average share of the wife’s labor income in total household income over the period 1969–1971.10

I control for state-year fixed effectsαstto absorb aggregate and state-specific trends, for example, in house prices (cf.Offer 2007). Moreover, I exploit the panel dimension of the PSID by including household fixed effectsγito absorb all household characteristics that are constant over time.Xistis a vector of time-varying demographic controls.11In particular, I include dummies for age group and the number of children to control for life cycle patterns, as well as income group dummies.12Standard errors are clustered at the household and state levels.

The coefficients of interest are those on the interaction term,βt⁠. Given the previous discussion, it can be expected that households profited all the more from the reform the more the wife contributed to the household’s income. The idea is thus to use households with a lower pre-reform female income share as a control group for those with a higher share. I group households based on the pre-reform income share because it is predetermined with respect to the reform, such that group membership stays fixed over time.13The identifying assumption is that (conditional on covariates) households with a higher as opposed to a lower pre-reform female income share would have been on a common trend in the outcome variable if the reform had not happened. One may also estimate a more coarse but slightly simplified version of Equation (1), using a binary indicator as the interaction variable and thus comparing households with a positive to those with a zero pre-reform female income share. I will present results from this simplified version of the model below in Section3.2.1, and use them to quantify the aggregate effects of the reform.

As a first outcome, I consider the housing DTI ratio inFigure 4, panel A, which plots the coefficientsβtover time.14Before 1971, housing DTI ratios evolved similarly for all married households, no matter how much the wife’s earnings contributed to total household income. Yet after the ECOA, households increased their housing DTI ratios all the more the larger the wife’s pre-reform income share was. The effect is persistent and remains significant at the 95%level until the mid-1980s.Table 1presents difference-in-differences estimates obtained by replacing the year dummies in Equation (1) with a dummy for the period after 1971. The point estimate for the DTI ratio implies that a household in which the wife’s average pre-reform income share was 1-percentage-point higher increased its housing DTI ratio by around 0.21 percentage points (column 1) after the reform. Correspondingly, a 30-percentage-point increase in the wife’s income share translates into a 6-percentage-point (⁠30×0.21⁠) increase in DTI, which is approximately 16%of the average in-sample DTI ratio of 36.55%⁠.

Home equity extraction, that is, additional borrowing of incumbent owners against their home, only became popular and widespread in the 1980s (seeBartscher et al. 2021and references therein). It is thus likely that the relative increase in housing DTI ratios of households with a high female income contribution is at most partially driven by additional borrowing of existing owners. Consistently, the effect operates through the extensive margin. This means that DTI ratios increase because more households take out mortgages, and not because existing borrowers increase their mortgage balance.Figure 4, panel B, shows the dynamic effects at the extensive margin, which are highly significant and persistent. The fact that we see a persistent relative increase in the borrowing rate speaks against the view that the reform forced lenders to extend credit to risky borrowers with systematically higher default probabilities (see Section1). In this case, we should only observe a short-lived increase, which would soon be reversed.

The clear visual pattern inFigure 4, panel B, translates into a strongly significant point estimate inTable 1.15A 1-percentage-point higher female income contribution leads to a 0.3-percentage-points higher probability of having housing debt (column 3). By contrast, the coefficient for the logarithm of the DTI ratio in column 2, which captures the intensive margin, is small, insignificant, and actually negative. As the relative share of households with positive housing debt increases among households with a high female income contribution, the relative share of households making mortgage payments increases as well. This leads to a highly significant point estimate for the debt-service-to-income (DSTI) ratio in column 4, while there is no effect at the intensive margin (column 5).Figure 4, panel C, confirms that also for the DSTI ratio, there were no pre-trends before the reform.

Absent home equity extraction, there are two other plausible reasons for increases in the extensive margin of housing debt. First, households may rely less on other sources of home financing, for example, borrowing from their family (cf.Del Boca and Lusardi 2003). Second, the homeownership rate may increase.Figure 5, panel B, confirms that homeownership has indeed increased more for households with a high female income contribution. This relative increase at the extensive margin has led to a corresponding relative increase in HTI ratios (seeFigure 5, panel A).Table 2contains the related difference-in-differences point estimates. The average HTI ratio rises by around 0.3 percentage points more for every additional percentage point of the wife’s average pre-reform income share (column 1), and the likelihood of homeownership increases by 0.2 percentage points (column 3).

Like the debt effect, the effect on housing mainly works through the extensive margin. The coefficient for the logarithm of the HTI ratio in the second column ofTable 2is positive, but very small and not significant. By construction, the LTV ratio is only defined for homeowners, and thus does not reflect changes at the extensive margin of housing. Together with the small negative effect at the intensive margin of housing debt, the small positive effect at the intensive margin of housing translates into a negative LTV effect (column 4). Finally, I find that households with a higher female income contribution increase the size of their home by more after the reform (seeFigure 5, panel C, and column 5 ofTable 2). This effect is mainly driven by households moving from a smaller rented to a larger owned property.16A few households also upgrade to larger homes, in line with the small positive intensive margin effect.

The main focus of this paper is on married women, because they had the greatest difficulties to obtain credit in the pre-reform era based on the historical evidence, and accounted for a substantially larger share of mortgagors than singles. However, singles may also have benefited from the reform, given that it precluded credit discrimination related to both sex and marital status. To investigate whether their access to credit improved as well, I estimated similar regressions for singles (seeInternet Appendix C).Figure C.3in theInternet Appendixshows that singles indeed borrowed more subsequent to the ECOA, compared to married households with a nonworking wife. However, there is no evidence of an associated increase in homeownership. A possible interpretation is that singles could substitute away from other financial sources, such as loans or transfers from relatives (cf.Del Boca and Lusardi 2003), which are generally less important for married couples.Figure C.4in theInternet Appendixshows that the responses were similar for single men and single women.

3.2.1 Translation into aggregate effects

How do the estimated effects at the micro level translate into changes in homeownership and borrowing at the macro level? To quantify the effects on overall mortgage borrowing and homeownership, I replaceshareiprein Equation (1) with a binary indicator equal to one ifshareipreis positive, and the year dummies with a dummy equal to one after 1971. This allows me to directly compute the total effect on households with working wives.17Moreover, the estimates from the binary specification can be compared more easily to the effect size under the alternative event study identification in Section3.2.2. The point estimates for the borrowing and homeownership rates are summarized inTable 3, and the dynamic effects are shown inFigure B.2in theInternet Appendix. The point estimate of around 0.08 for the extensive margin of mortgage debt, multiplied by the average share of married households with a working wife among all households in 1971 (⁠≈34.4%⁠) and the total number of households in this year (⁠≈64,778,000) yields a number of 1.8 million additional borrowers. The analogous calculation for homeownership implies that 1.4 million couples were enabled to buy their own home.

As discussed byCuomo (1981), the ECOA did not abolish all restrictions in credit access for women. For instance, married women without an own income could still not obtain credit without their husband’s approval in separate property states. However, the ECOA provided more comprehensive protection to working wives earning their own income. My results show that this translated into sizable and significant positive effects on access to credit for working wives and their households. In particular, more couples could take out mortgages to become homeowners and increase the size of their home. In the following, I will examine the robustness of my baseline findings.

3.2.2 Robustness

The results of the national-level difference-in-differences regressions are consistent with plausible effects of the ECOA. However, they might still, at least partly, capture effects of other contemporaneous events that might have affected households with working wives differentially. To lend further credibility to my results, I assess their robustness to control strategies addressing plausible candidates for potential confounding factors. In particular, I look at the entrance of the baby boomer cohort into housing markets, portfolio shifts related to the Great Inflation, the Equal Employment Opportunity Act, the 1973 oil crisis, changes in birth control and divorce laws, bank branching deregulation, the end of the Vietnam War, and the introduction of the Earned Income Tax Credit. Moreover, I explore alternative variable definitions and placebo reforms.Sections B.4andB.5in theInternet Appendixpresent the details.

While these additional tests confirm a high level of robustness, the institutional setup allows me to provide even stronger evidence based on state-level variation. As described in Section1, some U.S. states already introduced laws against discrimination before the ECOA became effective at the federal level. This setup opens the possibility to exploit variation across states for identification. Such an approach is much more robust to potential confounding events, because these events would have to occur both in the same states and at the same time as the introduction of anti-lending-discrimination laws.

In the national-level difference-in-differences regressions, the effects are identified from a comparison between households with different female income contributions (or households with versus without a working wife in the binary case) before and after the reform. While the pre-trends are flat and insignificant, and I include demographic controls and household fixed effects, one may still be worried that households with a working wife are not fully comparable to those with a nonworking wife for potentially unobservable reasons. A further advantage of the event study design is that it allows me to address this concern by comparing households with a working wife across states. Specifically, I consider households in which the wife had a positive average labor income over the three periods prior to the event, and compare their outcomes between treated and untreated states before and after the event. Consequently, the identification now comes from a comparison of households with a working wife in states which have already introduced anti-discrimination legislation in a given year to similar households with a working wife in states which have not introduced such legislation yet.18The identifying assumption is that without any reforms, the considered households would have been on the same trend in the outcome across states (conditional on covariates).

I collected information on the relevant state laws and use it to estimate event study regressions and assess whether my previous findings are consistent with those obtained from this alternative identification strategy. Because of data availability, I focus on the housing outcomes.19According to theU.S. Department of Labor.(1975, p. 384), 40 states had “legislation or regulations on some aspect of discrimination in credit based on sex and/or marital status” as of April 1975.Table B.2in theInternet Appendixprovides an overview over these laws. States which introduced anti-credit-discrimination legislation earlier might have witnessed effects on homeownership earlier. To test this hypothesis, I estimate event study regressions similar to the specification in Equation (1):

Recent work in econometrics has shown that for difference-in-differences regressions with variation in treatment timing, the estimation with two-way fixed effects may be biased in the presence of heterogeneous treatment effects (seeGoodman-Bacon 2021;Sun and Abraham 2021, and the references therein). Treatment effects may unfold dynamically over time under the assumption of homogenous treatment effects, but they may not differ across different treatment cohorts (Schmidheiny and Siegloch 2020;Sun and Abraham 2021).24in theInternet Appendixshows that the results obtained with the “interaction-weighted estimator” ofSun and Abraham (2021), which is robust to heterogeneous treatment effects, are very similar to those obtained from estimating a comparable model with two-way fixed effects. All in all, the fact that the effects obtained from the alternative event study identification align well with those found in the difference-in-differences setup lends support to a causal interpretation of the national-level results.

3.3 Effects on female labor supply

After having studied the effects on housing and mortgage debt, my second aim is to shed light on the potential labor supply effects of the ECOA. Upon its introduction, the reform primarily affected households in which the wife had already been working, given that banks were still allowed to take employment continuity and income stability into account. However, the new legislation might also have incentivized women to work more in the subsequent years in order to benefit from the new lending rules. The theoretical effects on labor supply are ex ante ambiguous. To buy a house of a given quality and size, a woman can ceteris paribus afford to work less if 100%instead of 50%of her income are considered. On the other hand, an additional dollar of income now translates into a higher borrowing capacity one-for-one, making female labor supply more attractive.

Consider the stylized example of a woman with an earnings potential of $30. Her husband currently earns $30, and their housing preferences are such that a suitable home would at least require a mortgage of $120. With a maximum DTI ratio of 2, they would need at least $60 of income. As long as the couple can only borrow against half of the wife’s income, that is, $15, they will still not be able to buy their desired home even if the wife is working. Yet once her full income can be used, there is a strong incentive for her to take up work.

Figure 7shows the results of event studies for the wife’s probability to work more than 500 hours per year, which corresponds to a quarter of full-time employment. The left panel considers all married households with a wife of “prime working age” (below 55).25The point estimates suggest an increase in workforce participation of 2 to 3 percentage points among married women in treatment compared to control states after the reform. However, the coefficients are estimated with relatively low precision.

The right panel focuses on a subsample of households for which theoretical and empirical considerations would suggest particularly strong effects. This sample includes young households (below 35) who were renters before the reform, and whose wife was working less than 500 hours. The choice is motivated by the fact that employability declines with age, such that older women will be less likely to join the workforce. Moreover, the probability of already being a homeowner increases with age, and households typically buy their first home before their mid-thirties. The empirical analyses however showed that the introduction of anti-lending discrimination laws was particularly important for households who did not own a home yet.Figure 7, panel B, suggests that in this subsample, the labor supply reaction of women in treatment states was indeed stronger, and the point estimates are significantly different from zero at several horizons. However, note that the restricted sample is relatively small (191 households), such that the results should be interpreted carefully.26in theInternet Appendixconfirms that the restricted sample of households with a high propensity to react is indeed driving the overall results. It further shows that similar patterns emerge when using female labor income or hours worked as the outcome, although the coefficients are estimated with varying precision.

In summary, the data suggest that younger women in households aspiring to purchase a home responded to the reform by working more. It seems plausible that wives in couples that only formed after the reform reacted in a similar way. Yet while we can observe changes in behavior around a reform for already-existing couples in the data, we cannot observe if a couple that founded a household after the reform would have made different female labor supply and housing decisions if the reform had never happened. To examine whether a woman at the beginning of her economic life cycle would make different plans for her financial and professional future in the post-reform world compared to a world in which there never is a reform, I use a structural model of households’ homeownership and borrowing over the life cycle. With the model, I can implement the “ideal” version of the experiment that the natural experiment in the data is meant to approximate. In particular, I can simulate two alternative paths of history which differ only in one aspect, namely, whether or not the wife’s income is discounted in a mortgage application. A comparison of these two worlds then allows me to study the causal effects of the reform. The model thus serves to compute counterfactuals that remain unobserved in the data, and to assess the plausibility of the empirical estimates from a theoretical perspective.
article.4. Life Cycle Model:
To obtain a “laboratory” to conduct the experiment described in the previous section, I will build a simple model inspired by previous work in the life cycle literature. Based on a life cycle model calibrated to data from the British Household Panel Survey (BHPS) for the period 1991 to 2002,Bottazzi, Low, and Wakefield (2007)show that the empirical correlation between large housing debt and longer female hours worked can be generated by the requirement to meet mortgage obligations. They further show that a tightening of the DTI constraint, which operates on both male and female income in their model, leads to delayed home purchases. They do not report effects on female labor force participation (FLFP), but note that they found small effects.Pizzinelli (2018)calibrates a similar two-earner model to BHPS data from 1991 to 2008, and allows the income of the secondary earner to influence the DTI constraint in a different way than the primary earner’s income. In his specification, the DTI constraint always depends on full-time earnings, that is, what the secondary earner would earn if working full time. He simulates a relaxation of this constraint by increasing the multiplier on the secondary earner’s full-time labor income, and finds a positive labor supply effect. His results further suggest that the secondary earner’s LFP response can amplify the increase in homeownership.

After describing the model and my strategy to calibrate it to the early 1970s in the following subsections, I will use it as a laboratory to study the effects of abolishing income discounting on housing, credit, and labor market outcomes.

4.1 Model description

My goal is to examine from a theoretical perspective whether married women changed their financial and career planning in response to the reform. To do so, I build a simple life cycle model with borrowing constraints. Households are formed at age j = 25 ⁠ . Both spouses retire at age 65, and die at age 80. They maximize their utility over consumption c j ⁠ , female labor supply n j f and housing h j ⁠ : u ( c j , n j f , h j ) = c j 1 − σ 1 − σ + θ f ( j ) ( 1 − n j f ) 1 − ψ 1 − ψ I j ≤ 65 + [ χ ( j ) μ h + ϕ h h j − h ― h ― − h ― ] I h > 0 .

The parameterσdetermines the degree of risk aversion.θf(j)determines the strength of preferences for a working-age wife’s time spent on activities other than market work, which can vary over the life cycle to reflect changes in the disutility of working, for example, induced by the presence of small children in the household or the need to take care of grandchildren.ψgoverns the elasticity of female labor supply. The strength of basic housing preferences depends on the parameterμh⁠, andϕhdetermines the preference for a larger house. Households can buy houses of a smaller sizeh―=1or a larger sizeh―=2⁠. Following a suggestion ofDruedahl (2015), preferences for owning versus renting are allowed to vary over the life cycle, reflecting factors such as changing mobility preferences and changes in demand for space. I model these time-varying preferences in a reduced-form way, similar toPizzinelli (2018), by premultiplyingμhwith an age-dependent factorχ(j)(see Section4.2andInternet Appendix D.2for details).

The logarithm of hourly wages, w j s ⁠ , is modeled as the sum of a deterministic function of age and an autoregressive process: l n ( w j s ) = α 0 s + α 1 s j + α 2 s j 2 + α 3 s j 3 + α 4 s j 4 + l n ( z j s ) , s = m , f l n ( z j s ) = ρ s l n ( z j − 1 s ) + ε j s , ε j s ∼ N ( − σ ε s 2 2 , σ ε s 2 ) . (3)

Both spouses’ wages are subject to idiosyncratic risk. Men always have a standard full-time contract, corresponding to 40 hours per week. Women can choose whether to work or not, and whether to work full- or part-time. Specifically, they can choose to work 20, 30, 40, or 50 hours a week. In other words, they choose between discrete contracts in the setN={0,20/T,30/T,40/T,50/T}⁠, which expressnjfas hours worked relative to the total number of nonsleeping hours per weekT=7×16=112⁠.27Working less than 40 hours a week is associated with a part-time penaltyP⁠. After age 65, both spouses receive a retirement income based on the replacement rateb⁠. The retirement income is calculated asbtimes the realization of the wage process in the last preretirement period.28

Households can save in a risk-free financial asset a j at the interest rate r = r s ⁠ , or borrow d j = − a j at a rate of r = r b ⁠ . This implies that they cannot hold both positive financial assets and mortgage debt at the same time, as in Attanasio et al. (2012) and Pizzinelli (2018) . 29 Borrowing is limited by the minimum of an LTV and DTI constraint: ϕ ( h j , y j m , y j f ) = m i n { λ h p h j , λ y ( y j m + λ d y j f ) } , (4) where λ h is the LTV limit and p is the house price. This implies three options to choose from: (a) the household does not own a house, in which case asset holdings must be positive; (b) the household owns a home, but the LTV constraint ( ⁠ λ h p h j ≥ d j + 1 ⁠ ) binds before the DTI constraint; or (c) the household owns a home, and the DTI constraint ( ⁠ λ y ( y j m + λ d y j f ) ≥ d j + 1 ⁠ ) binds before the LTV constraint.

Mortgages are modeled as long-term debt, meaning that households must only fulfill the LTV and DTI constraint upon origination of the mortgage (Attanasio et al. 2012;Kaplan, Mitman, and Violante 2020). An alternative modeling option would be to impose a DSTI instead of a DTI constraint. However, the DSTI and DTI ratio are directly proportionate for a mortgage with fixed duration and interest rate, as shown inInternet Appendix D.1.30Formulating the constraint in terms of debt rather than debt service allows a direct comparison to the LTV constraint, as illustrated inFigure D.1in theInternet Appendix.

I followAttanasio et al. (2012)and require that households at least pay the interestrddjon their outstanding debt. As shown inInternet Appendix D.1, this is equivalent to the constraintdj+1≤dj⁠. Since the model is meant to describe the early 1970s, when home equity extraction was still uncommon, households are not allowed to extract home equity.31Households close to or in retirement (above 60 years) cannot take out new debt but can continue to repay their existing debt. This impliesϕ(⋅)=0∀j≥60⁠.

As it is not the goal of this paper to model debt and homeownership in old age, I abstract from bequests.32In the last period of life, households are required to pay all outstanding debt and consume what is left of their wealth. Households choosecj⁠,aj+1⁠,njf⁠, andhj⁠, given the current vector of statesXj=[aj,hj−1,wjm,wjf]⁠. The model is in partial equilibrium. 4.2 Calibration and model fit

I calibrate the model to the early 1970s, and investigate the effects of relaxing the DTI constraint based on the creditable share of the wife’s income.

Externally calibrated parameters.The LTV and DTI limits are set toλh=1andλy=2⁠, which corresponds to the 90th percentile of the LTV and DTI distributions for new homeowners in the baseline sample between 1969 and 1971. House values and net rental costs are chosen based on the same data. The rental cost is set to the median rental cost of around $5,300. The value of the “entry-level” house is set to the average house value among households between ages 25 and 35, which is approximately $110,000. The value of the larger house is set to match the average for households aged 40 to 50 (around $135,000), which is the period when life cycle house values peak in the data. To calibrate the mortgage interest rate, I use the SCF+ from 1969 to 1971. Specifically, I compute the median mortgage interest rate of homeowners aged 25 to 64 who bought a home in the current or previous year and have an interest-bearing mortgage, which is 6.3%⁠. The savings interest rate is set to the annual three-month treasury bill rate published by the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (FRED series TB3MS), which was 4.3%in 1971. The CRRA parameterσis set to the common value of 2, and the discount factorβis chosen as 0.94. The pension replacement rate is set to 0.7, as inAttanasio et al. (2012). An overview of the externally calibrated parameters is given in panel A ofTable 4.

(2018). Wage processes.I estimate the wage processes from Equation (3) based on my baseline sample of married households, separately for husbands and wives. Following a common approach in the literature, men and women working above 500 hours per year are considered as working. I regress the logarithm of real hourly wages on a quadratic polynomial in age, as well as dummies for the number of children, state, and 5-year cohorts. For the wives, I apply a Heckman model, in which selection is based on household income net of the wife’s labor income, number of children, and household size. The interceptsα0sare determined by averages for the cohort born between 1945 and 1950, who were of typical “home-buying age” in the early 1970s. As women can work part-time in the model, I allow for part-time penaltiesP50andP75by including dummies for working 50%or 75%⁠. Following the previous literature, the parameterρsis set to one for both spouses. The variancesσεs2,s=m,fare estimated according to the method ofHeathcote, Perri, and Violante (2010).34Table 6shows the estimated coefficients, andFigure D.1in theInternet Appendixplots the resultant income profiles under certainty.

The first column shows the responses when female labor supply is kept fixed at the optimal choices under the no-reform scenario. More precisely, I compute conditional policy functions for each possible female labor supply choice under the reform scenario withλd=1⁠. In the simulations, I then use the realized trajectory ofnjffrom the simulation under the no-reform scenario withλd=0.5⁠, and compute the asset and housing choices based on the conditional policy functions corresponding to these “no-reform” labor supply choices in each period. In other words, I simulate a scenario in which women can use their full income for the mortgage, but they do not change their labor supply relative to the no-reform scenario. This “restricted” experiment is meant to approximate the empirical effects on households in which the wife had already made her labor supply decisions in the no-reform world.

The second column ofTable 7shows the responses when female labor supply is allowed to freely adapt once income discounting is abolished. In this “unrestricted” experiment, the homeownership and borrowing rates of married households increase by around 9 percentage points. Under the “restricted” scenario, the increases occur because households can borrow against a higher share of what the wife would have earned anyway. Yet in line with the suggestive evidence from the data, the reform incentivizes women to take up work. In the third row ofTable 7, we can see that the FLFP rate increases by around 2 percentage points.35Figure 9shows that this is again very similar to the effect found in the data, although the latter is estimated imprecisely. The model thus suggests that the medium- to long-run effects of the ECOA on homeownership and borrowing even exceeded the effects on impact by encouraging more married women to work.

At the intensive margin, female labor supply changes little, with women working about 23 minutes less per week on average. This is because on the one hand, the women who would also have worked in the no-reform scenario withλd=0.5choose to work slightly less (around 19 minutes per week). On the other hand, the women who join the labor force under the reform scenario, but would not have worked without the reform, work somewhat less than the average (23.6 compared to 28 hours per week).

Figure 10plots the effects of the reform under the “unrestricted” scenario over the life cycle. The bars represent averages by age group. Consistent with the theoretical arguments discussed above, the effects are strongest for young households between 25 and 34 years, and decline as households become older. For households in the young group, who are of typical “homebuying age,” the wives’ LFP increases by around 4.5 percentage points. The ability to count twice as much of the wife’s income toward the mortgage as before, combined with the active increase in FLFP, enables households to buy a home earlier in life, such that the homeownership rate in the youngest group increases most drastically.

Finally, I decompose the results inFigure 10by husbands’ life cycle incomes, as represented by the dotted and squared markers. In line with the empirical results and theoretical considerations, the reform bears the greatest advantages for households with lower-income husbands (dots). For these households, the effect is higher even at later stages of the life cycle. While young households benefit from the reform even if the husband has above-median life cycle earnings (squares), the average effects at older ages are mostly driven by households with below-median male earnings. However, the effects on FLFP are negligible for both groups of households in the second half of the working-age period, in line with the empirical estimates being concentrated among young households.

The model suggests that women who were planning their future after the prohibition of income discounting in the early 1970s were, based on the prevailing economic information at that time, more likely to embark on a working career.36It further illustrates that additional female labor supply could substantially amplify the positive effects of equal credit opportunity laws on married couples’ homeownership rates in the longer run, especially for younger couples and those with lower male earnings.
article.5. Conclusion:
This paper has shed light on the effects of the Equal Credit Opportunity Act on homeownership and mortgage borrowing of married households. The ECOA prohibited discrimination in mortgage lending based on sex and marital status, in particular the practice to discount the wife’s income in couples’ joint mortgage applications. It hence provides a natural experiment to study the relaxation of income-related borrowing constraints.

Using data from the PSID, I find that couples with a working wife increased their mortgage borrowing, and with it homeownership and house size, after the passage of the ECOA. The estimates imply that the legislation enabled 1.4 million married households to move to an own home upon its introduction. Results at the federal level are supported by event study regressions exploiting state-level variation.

The law initially mostly benefited households in which the wife had already been working, but it also changed the labor supply incentives for women in future cohorts of new homeowners. I draw on a life cycle model to substantiate suggestive evidence from the data and explore potential changes in married women’s labor supply. The model buttresses the view that the ECOA incentivized married women to take up work, thereby amplifying the positive effects on married couples’ homeownership and borrowing rates.

Taken together, my results suggest that countries can benefit from improving women’s financial inclusion, as this provides incentives for women to work and allows their households to accumulate wealth, and to start doing so earlier in life. This is especially true for developing and emerging countries (cf.Hyland, Djankov, and Goldberg 2020;Field et al. 2021), which make up the largest share of the countries that still do not have explicit laws against gender-based discrimination in credit access, according to the World Bank’s WBL database.

Strengthening women’s inclusion in financial and labor markets may also have important effects for within-household bargaining and insurance. For instance, the increased ability to rely on wives’ incomes for mortgage borrowing after the ECOA might have had positive effects on within-household insurance against negative income shocks in the context of the diffusion of home-equity-based borrowing since the 1980s (Bartscher et al. 2021;Hurst and Stafford 2004;Herkenhoff, Phillips, and Cohen-Cole 2020). Moreover, it is plausible that women gained more say in their household’s financial decisions once their income became more relevant for borrowing (Addoum, Kung, and Morales 2016;Guiso and Zaccaria 2021). These aspects may provide interesting avenues for further research.
